skill.name: video-extend
skill.description: Extends an existing video clip through the RunComfy CLI using Google Veo 3-1 extend endpoints. It helps choose the standard or fast endpoint, shape a continuation prompt, and save the generated clip.
skill.summary: Continue existing video clips with RunComfy and Google Veo 3-1.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown backticks, endpoint names, and ordinary prose. The confirmed risk is medium because the community skill directs agents to use an external CLI, send media prompts to RunComfy, and rely on token storage for authentication.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Required
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access for runcomfy commands and provides install, login, and run examples. This is expected for the skill, but it depends on a third-party CLI and should remain constrained to the documented runcomfy commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples directly show runcomfy CLI use. The context frames it as legitimate model invocation, not arbitrary shell execution.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Processing
medium_findings.description: The skill routes source video URLs and continuation prompts to RunComfy model endpoints. This is core functionality, but users should understand that media references and prompts leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill names RunComfy endpoints and states that the CLI POSTs to the RunComfy Model API. The data transfer is explicit and central to the workflow.

medium_findings.title: API Token Handling and Local Token Storage
medium_findings.description: The workflow uses runcomfy login or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN, and documents token storage under a hidden config path. This is normal authentication behavior, but tokens must not be exposed in prompts, logs, or shared environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly mention login, environment token use, and local token storage. The skill also warns not to echo tokens, which lowers malicious intent concerns.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Video Content Can Influence Outputs
medium_findings.description: The skill recognizes source video URLs as untrusted and warns that embedded text, EXIF, or hidden instructions can influence continuation results. This is an indirect prompt injection risk for agents using third-party media.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk is explicitly documented in the skill. It affects output behavior, but no instruction in the file attempts to override this audit.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many inline code spans and command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. No Ruby source or dynamic backtick execution is present in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown prose, links, or code spans. The real executable surface is the documented runcomfy CLI, covered separately.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography False Positive
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography findings appear to match ordinary words and headings, not cryptographic algorithm usage. No evidence found of DES, MD5, SHA-1, or custom cryptography in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain description text, prompting advice, and an exit-codes heading. There is no cryptographic API, hash function, or cipher configuration.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links and Catalog URLs
low_findings.description: Most hardcoded URLs are RunComfy homepage, model catalog, CLI documentation, and related skill links. These are expected references, though the service endpoints still create the network risk described above.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to RunComfy pages, documentation, and marketplace skill references. No unrelated webhook, paste site, or suspicious exfiltration host was found.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access Is Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The hidden config path is documented as the RunComfy CLI token location with mode 0600. The skill does not instruct the agent to read or exfiltrate that file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line explains credential storage and includes a warning not to expose tokens. No command accesses the token file directly.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Execution Required
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash access for runcomfy commands and provides install, login, and run examples. This is expected for the skill, but it depends on a third-party CLI and should remain constrained to the documented runcomfy commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples directly show runcomfy CLI use. The context frames it as legitimate model invocation, not arbitrary shell execution.

dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly mention login, environment token use, and local token storage. The skill also warns not to echo tokens, which lowers malicious intent concerns.

content.value_statement: Short generated clips often stop before the story beat is complete. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code extend an existing video through RunComfy and Veo 3-1.
actual_capabilities: Selects Veo 3-1 Extend or Fast Extend based on quality and cost intent.
Builds the runcomfy run workflow with a source video URL and continuation prompt.
Gives prompting guidance for camera anchors, one-beat extensions, and chained shots.
Explains common exit codes for bad input, authentication failures, timeouts, and upstream errors.
Documents token handling, endpoint allowlists, shell boundaries, and generated file size limits.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, an authenticated account, and access to the Veo 3-1 endpoints.
Extends existing video clips; it does not create a first clip from scratch.
Sends video URLs and prompts to RunComfy for remote processing.
Long chains can accumulate identity drift after several extensions.

use_cases.title: Finish a Short Product Clip
use_cases.description: Extend an approved product shot so the final movement has enough time for a campaign edit.

use_cases.title: Draft Narrative Beats
use_cases.description: Chain several short continuations from one seed clip while keeping each extension focused on one action.

use_cases.title: Automate Video Continuation Tests
use_cases.description: Use repeatable RunComfy CLI calls to compare standard and fast extension outputs during workflow testing.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Clip Extension
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend this video using the standard Veo 3-1 endpoint. Keep the camera steady and continue the main action naturally.

prompt_templates.title: Fast Draft Extension
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Fast Extend for this clip. Continue only the next story beat and save the result for review.

prompt_templates.title: Controlled Camera Move
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend the clip with a slow dolly out. The subject turns toward the window, then pauses in soft daylight.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Beat Chain Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan three consecutive extensions from this source clip. Keep each beat short, name the endpoint, and warn me about drift risk.

output_examples.input: A user provides an eight-second product clip and asks for a longer ending.
output_examples.output: The assistant chooses the standard extend endpoint, writes a one-beat continuation prompt, and saves the finished clip for review.

output_examples.input: A producer wants quick options before paying for final quality.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends Fast Extend for drafts, then standard Extend once the preferred motion is selected.

output_examples.input: A developer asks why a video continuation failed after submission.
output_examples.output: The assistant maps the reported exit code to likely causes such as bad input, authentication failure, timeout, or upstream error.

best_practices: Use one clear action beat per extension to reduce motion and identity drift.
Keep API tokens in the CLI login store or environment variables, not in prompts.
Use Fast Extend for drafts and the standard endpoint for final delivery.
anti_patterns: Do not ask the skill to generate the first video from only text or an image.
Do not paste RunComfy tokens into chat messages, logs, or shared documents.
Do not chain many extensions without reviewing drift after each generated clip.

faq.question: What does this skill extend?
faq.answer: It extends an existing video clip by sending the clip reference and a continuation prompt to RunComfy.

faq.question: Does it create videos from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on continuation. Use a text-to-video or image-to-video skill for the first clip.

faq.question: Which endpoint should I use?
faq.answer: Use Fast Extend for drafts and standard Extend when quality matters more than speed or cost.

faq.question: Does it require a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow needs the RunComfy CLI and authentication through login or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN.

faq.question: Will my video stay private?
faq.answer: The video URL and prompt are processed by RunComfy. Review your data policy before using sensitive media.

faq.question: Can it make very long videos?
faq.answer: It can chain extensions, but identity and motion drift can accumulate after several generations.
